slides.title: Don't Reinvent the Wheel
slides.body: A **module** is a ready-made `.py` file full of useful code. Instead of writing everything yourself, you `import` it and put it straight to work.
slides.tip: Python ships with hundreds of built-in modules — they're called the standard library.

slides.title: import a Whole Toolbox
slides.body: Use `import math` to load the whole module. Then reach inside with a **dot** to grab any tool you need.

slides.title: Grab Just What You Need
slides.body: Use `from math import sqrt` to pull in **one specific item** — no prefix required when you call it.
slides.code:
from math import sqrt, pi

print(sqrt(49))
print(pi)
slides.tip: Use this style when you only need one or two things from a module.

slides.title: Give Modules a Nickname
slides.body: Long module names? Use `import ... as` to create a **short alias** — this is standard practice with popular libraries.
slides.code:
import math as m

print(m.sqrt(16))
print(m.ceil(2.1))
slides.tip: You'll often see numpy as np and pandas as pd in data science code.

slides.title: The if __name__ Guard
slides.body: Code at the top level of a module runs on **every import**. Wrap test code in `if __name__ == "__main__":` so it only runs when you execute the file directly.
slides.tip: When imported, __name__ is the filename; when run directly, it's "__main__".

slides.title: You've Got the Import Pattern!
slides.body: You can now `import` built-in modules, grab specific items with `from ... import`, create short aliases, and protect code with `if __name__ == "__main__":`. **Every `.py` file you write is already a module.**
